coredata.dc:title: Insight into the CO2 photoreduction mechanism over 9-hydroxyphenal-1-one (HPHN) carbon quantum dots

Zhang, Heming

Shi, Yantao
Si, Duanhui
Li, Hongjiang
Hao, Ce
coredata.dc:description: Converting CO2 to carbon-containing fuels is an effective approach to relieving energy shortages. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have shown distinct properties and attracted tremendous interest in CO2 reduction. Herein, we report a joint experimental-computational mechanistic study of photoreduction CO2 to CO on the model catalyst 9-hydroxyphenal-1-one (HPHN) CQDs with known structure. Our theoretical calculations reveal that the rate-determining step is COOH∙ formation, which is closely related to the proton and electron transfer induced by hydrogen bonding in the excited state. According to the calculated volcano plot, the solution we proposed is addition Zn2+ ions. The active center changed from the hydroxyl oxygen atom to the Zn atom and the barrier of the COOH∙ formation step is noticeably decreased when Zn2+ ions are added. It is further confirmed by the experimental data that the activity of CO2 reduction increases 2.9 times when Zn2+ ions are added.

DFT study
Reaction mechanism
Hydrogen bonding
Excited state

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental characterization and CO2 photoreduction for HPHN-CQDs
Computational section of the photophysical and photochemical processes for Complex
Screening the best metal ions using volcano plot

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations of the proposed mechanism diagram for CO2 reduction to CO and the molecular and crystal structures of HPHN. Gray, red and white (pink) balls represent C, O and H atoms, respectively.

(a) TEM image of HPHN-CQDs (the inset is the particle size statistics), (b) the particle size distribution of HPHN-CQDs by DLS, (c) the zeta potential analysis of HPHN-CQDs, (d) UV–Vis absorption spectra of HPHN-CQDs in water and HPHN solution in ethanol, (e) Fluorescence spectra of HPHN-CQDs and emission images of HPHN-CQDs dispersed in water (right), (f) Times courses of CO evolution over HPHN-CQDs, HPHN-CQDs-Ni2+ and HPHN-CQDs-Zn2+, the reaction was continued for 10 h.

(a) The most stable configuration of hydrogen-bonded complex, denoted as Complex. HB-1: C1=O2⋅⋅⋅H9-O10; HB-2: C1=O3⋅⋅⋅H5-O4; HB-3: O4-H6⋅⋅⋅O8=C7; (b) The diagram of photophysical process. S0: ground state; S1: excited singlet state; T1: excited triplet state.
The photochemical reaction path (Path-1) of CO2 reduction for Complex. Values in kcal/mol are given relative to compound 3R*.
Computed photocatalytic cycle for CO2 reduction on HPHN catalyst.
(a) The volcano map of Ionization potential (IP) for nine metals; (b) The reaction pathway of the COOH∙ formation step for Complex-Zn.
Relevant reactions (1–8) in the photoreduction of CO2.
The rate constants of fluorescence emission (k F), internal conversion (k IC), intersystem crossing (k ISC) and photochemical reactions (k R) for complex.
The relevant bond lengths, bond angle of CO2 and ADCH charges in the process of 3R* → 3P*.

head.ce:title: Insight into the CO2 photoreduction mechanism over 9-hydroxyphenal-1-one (HPHN) carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, Liaoning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, Liaoning, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: HPHN-CQDs are used as the model catalyst to study the mechanism of photoreduction CO2 to CO with known molecular and crystal structures.

Converting CO2 to carbon-containing fuels is an effective approach to relieving energy shortages. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have shown distinct properties and attracted tremendous interest in CO2 reduction. Herein, we report a joint experimental-computational mechanistic study of photoreduction CO2 to CO on the model catalyst 9-hydroxyphenal-1-one (HPHN) CQDs with known structure. Our theoretical calculations reveal that the rate-determining step is COOH∙ formation, which is closely related to the proton and electron transfer induced by hydrogen bonding in the excited state. According to the calculated volcano plot, the solution we proposed is addition Zn2+ ions. The active center changed from the hydroxyl oxygen atom to the Zn atom and the barrier of the COOH∙ formation step is noticeably decreased when Zn2+ ions are added. It is further confirmed by the experimental data that the activity of CO2 reduction increases 2.9 times when Zn2+ ions are added.

ce:section.ce:para: Mimicking natural green plant photosynthesis, which employs abundant solar energy, conversion of CO2 into energy-rich chemicals, materials, and fuels, is a promising approach to reducing the CO2 emission level and alleviating the energy crisis simultaneously [1–4]. This field has received increased attention, and significant efforts have been devoted to this field. To date, a number of representative photocatalysts [5–10] have been employed for CO2 reduction. However, the search for a better alternative has not ceased. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with a lateral size ≤20 nm [11,12], have been used as a class of promising candidates [13,14] for the photoreduction of CO2 due to their good optical absorption in the UV and near-visible regions, rich surface binding sites and electron transfer properties. Functionalized CQDs, such as semiconductors decorated with CQDs and CQDs doped with metal complexes [15–17], are usually applied as photocatalysts for CO2 conversion. Pristine CQDs as photocatalysts for CO2 conversion have seldom been reported.

Although functionalized CQDs can improve the catalytic activity of CO2 reduction, they still suffer from low efficiency. Thus, it is fundamentally important to explore the mechanism of CO2 reduction, which is highly challenging and still in the nascent stage [18–20]. CO2 reduction is energetically unfavorable due to its remarkable thermodynamic stability. This process requires a considerable transfer of energy and occurs in the electronically excited states. In addition, this process is a redox reaction involving electron transfer during the whole process and especially in the initiation step for CO2 activation [21–23], which is a key challenge and significantly critical for CO2 reduction. Third, this process is composed of a sequence of steps involving proton transfer, which finally determines the oxidation state of the carbon atom. Furthermore, the photocatalysts, CO2 and H2O may have the ability to form hydrogen bonds between each other, which is widely present in photoreduction CO2 systems and can form hydrogen-bonded systems [24–26]. According to the above four basic characteristics of CO2 reduction, the hydrogen bonds in the excited state may play an important role in CO2 reduction, related to the electron transfer and proton transfer, which is of great significance in understanding the mechanism of CO2 reduction. A complete understanding of the role of excited-state hydrogen bonds is indispensable and focused in this work.

In this study, metal-free 9-hydroxyphenal-1-one carbon quantum dots (HPHN-CQDs) were prepared as the model catalyst [27,28], which is quite appropriate for the mechanism research. HPHN-CQDs were applied as the photocatalyst for CO2 reduction, using water as the reductant, without any sacrificial agent or photosensitizer. The hydrogen-bonded complex formed by the photocatalyst, CO2 and H2O were constructed as the computational model of our research. Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) were applied to explore the photophysical and photochemical processes based on the hydrogen-bond complex in order to investigate the function of excited-state hydrogen bond in the electron transfer and proton transfer. The aim of this research is to understand the reaction mechanism of these hydrogen-bonded systems and to identify the rate-limiting step during the photophysical and photochemical processes. Furthermore, a theoretical prediction was proposed to screen the best metal ions by ionization potential (IP) to improve the photocatalytic efficiency, and then the corresponding verification was carried out by the calculation and the experiment.

All reagents were used as received without further purification. HPHN (97%) was yellow power and purchased from International Laboratory (IL, USA). Zinc chloride and nickel chloride hexahydrate were analytical grade reagents and purchased from Kermel Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

Firstly, a certain amount of HPHN uniformly dispersed in the deionized water and sonicated using an ordinary bath sonicator at 300 W for 2 h to attain a homogenous suspension. Then, a probe sonicator (SB-5200, Ningbo Xinzhi Bio-tech Co. Ltd) was used at 400 W for 5 min with the probe placed in the samples. After that, the samples were centrifuged for 30 min at 18000 rpm. Finally, the yellow HPHN-CQDs were obtained by carefully collecting the supernatants.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using a TECNAI F30 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI Company) operated at an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. The specimens for TEM analysis were prepared by depositing a drop of sample on a holey carbon-coated copper grid.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed with a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern Instruments Ltd., USA) to confirm the particles’ size distribution. The light source is a He-Ne gas laser (4 mW, λ 632.8 nm) in backscattering configuration (173°). Data were obtained at 25 °C. The DLS instrument used in this study can detect particle sizes ranging from 0.6 nm to 6000 nm. The zeta potential was also measured using the same apparatus by “mixed-mode measurement” phase analysis light scattering (M3-PALS) and laser doppler velocimetry. The Smoluchowski approximation for the Henry equation was used to calculate the zeta potential. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained on a Lambda750S. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Pro 97 (Shanghai, China).

In a typical process, 100 mL HPHN-CQDs as the photocatalyst was dispersed in a metal reactor with quartz plate, the volume of the reaction chamber was about 200 mL. For the preparation of the photocatalyst HPHN-CQDs-Zn2+ and HPHN-CQDs-Ni2+, we first prepared 0.01 M ZnCl2 and NiCl2·6H2O solutions, pipette out 1 mL and 1.5 mL of the above solutions into 100 mL HPHN-CQDs to get HPHN-CQDs-Zn2+ and HPHN-CQDs-Ni2+, respectively. The samples are evenly dispersed throughout the ultrasonic dispersion method mentioned above.

Before irradiation, high purity CO2 gas was bubbling into the reaction for two hours until all the gas was out. Then the light-irradiation experiment was performed by using a 300 W xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300) fitted with an attenuator filter to simulate one solar light illumination. During the reaction, 1.5 mL gas was extracted by a sampling needle from the reactor at given intervals (2 h) for subsequent concentration analysis. The produced gases (CO) were determined using gas chromatography (CEL-GC-7920) with N2 as the carrier gas. CO with low concentration was detected using a flame ionization detector (FID) equipped with methane conversion oven (carbon molecular sieve column), CO was converted to CH4 and then analyzed. The amount of products (CO) was calculated from the peak area using the standard calibration curves. The blank experiments (i.e., without photocatalyst, in the dark or in Ar atmosphere) were carried out and no carbon product was detected, confirming that CO2 is the only carbon source for CO product, as is shown in Table S1.

All the geometry optimizations and frequency calculations were performed with the Gaussian 09 program [29]. The hybrid functional of Becke-type three-parameter Lee-Yang-Parr (B3LYP) [30] was adopted to describe the correlation exchange functional. The basis sets were combined by full electrons basis of 6-31G (d,p) [31] for H, C, O atoms and the pseudopotential basis of Lanl2dz [32] with effective core potentials [33] (ECP) for the transition metal atoms. All calculations included the DFT-D3 method of Grimme with Becke-Johnson damping [34] (D3(BJ)). Additionally, the continuum solvation model [35] (SMD model) was employed to mimic the water solvent effect. The charges set in the calculation are 0.0 and 2.0 before and after the presence of the transition metals, respectively. The geometries of S0 and T1 states were calculated using DFT method at spin multiplicities of 1 and 3, respectively, which for S1 state, TDDFT method was applied. Since the photochemical reaction mainly occurs at the triplet state [36,37], the photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction was studied at the triplet state. The transition states (TS) were characterized by only one imaginary vibrational frequency. An intrinsic reaction coordinate (IRC) method was executed to verify each TS uniquely connects the reactants with products. In order to obtain more reliable and accurate results, the single-point energy calculations were used higher level (B3LYP/6-311+G(d,p) [38]/Lanl2dz) on the basis of the optimized structures.

The binding energy can be defined as: ΔE binding = E(total) − E(HPHN-metal) − E(CO2) − E(H2O), where E(total), E(HPHN-metal), E(CO2), and E(H2O) represent the total free energy of the hydrogen-bonded complex consist of HPHN-metal, CO2 and H2O, the free energy of HPHN-metal, CO2 and H2O, respectively.

Multiwfn program [39] was utilized to analyze the charge distributions using the atomic dipole moment-corrected Hirshfeld [40] (ADCH) method and the spin populations using the Mulliken [41] method. The rate constants of photophysical transitions were calculated by MOMAP program [42,43]. The detailed calculation processes are provided in the Supporting information (Computational section).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental characterization and CO2 photoreduction for HPHN-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The molecular and crystal structures of HPHN are shown in Fig. 1 , which is composed of only carbon, oxygen and hydrogen atoms. Specifically, HPHN has an electron-rich three-ring structure and an intramolecular hydrogen bond between the 1-carbonyl oxygen atom and the 9-hydroxy proton. The molecular structure of HPHN was obtained from its crystal structure [44] based on the information of the Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre; the CCDC deposition number is 1178988.

The size of the HPHN-CQDs was first characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 2 a), which shows a good sample preparation of the HPHN-CQDs in water and the particle size statistics show that the diameter is approximately 10 nm. Furthermore, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) in Fig. 2(b) indicates that the diameter of HPHN-CQDs is mainly centered at 21 nm, which is slightly higher than that from TEM due to the interference of the dispersant with the hydrodynamic diameter [45]. In addition, Fig. 2(c) shows that the zeta potential of the HPHN-CQDs is approximately −48.6 mV, which is measured in the water and the pH value is approximately 7. The zeta potential of HPHN-CQDs indicates that the repulsive forces between the particles are dominant and thus avoiding agglomeration; this repulsion resulted in HPHN-CQDs with good stability.

From Fig. 2(d) in the UV–Vis spectra, three typical absorption peaks are observed both for HPHN-CQDs in water and HPHN solution in ethanol, which absorbed in both the UV and visible regions. Compared with the UV–Vis spectra for HPHN solution in ethanol, a red shift is found in the UV–Vis spectra of the HPHN-CQDs in water. The oxygen-containing functional groups of the HPHN-CQDs are more likely to bind with water, which leads to more hydroxyl groups exposed on the surface, resulting in a red shift of the spectra. The fluorescence spectra of HPHN-CQDs was excited at 356 nm, and a strong emission peak at 451 nm can be observed in Fig. 2(e). The HPHN-CQDs exhibited blue emission with UV excitation.

To evaluate the effect of the HPHN-CQDs on CO2 photoreduction, we carried out a photocatalytic reaction in the presence of water as a reductant without any additional sacrificial agent and photosensitizer. As shown in Fig. 2(f), CO is the main product. O2 is difficult to desorb from the catalyst surface and we did not detect it, and no liquid products were detected. The yields of CO have a good linear relationship with the irradiation time. The mass of the added catalyst in 100 mL HPHN-CQDs is approximately 0.23 mg. (The detailed calculation process is provided in the Supporting information (Experimental section)). The total photocatalytic activity of HPHN-CQDs in 10 hours reached 58.1 μmol/g-cat. and reached an apparent quantum yield (AQY) of 5.3 × 10−4% (Fig. S1). Fig. 1 also presents the proposed mechanism diagram for CO2 reduction to CO on HPHN-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational section of the photophysical and photochemical processes for Complex
ce:section.ce:para: To further study the CO2 reduction process, we conducted DFT and TDDF calculations. The photocatalyst HPHN that we used has a precise experimental crystal structure. However, there are still no good solutions for the calculation of the excited states of periodic structures to date. In addition, the size of the HPHN-CQDs we prepared is about 20 nm, which is a cluster containing more than ten thousand HPHN molecules (Fig. S2) and is too large to be calculated in the excited state. Thus, we used a single HPHN molecular structure as the motif for our calculation, as shown in Fig. 1. We calculated the UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and IR spectra of the single HPHN molecular structure, as shown in Fig. S3. The calculated results are in good agreement with the experimental values, which indicates that our calculation model is reasonable.

In the systems of photocatalytic reduction of CO2, HPHN, CO2 and H2O molecules can form different hydrogen-bonded complexes at different binding sites. Molclus program [46] was adopted to search the possible binding configurations for H2O and CO2 molecules adsorbed on HPHN molecule. We optimized the different hydrogen-bonded complexes for HPHN-H2O, HPHN-CO2, HPHN-H2O-CO2, and HPHN-2H2O-CO2, respectively. Their detailed structures are given in Figs. S4-S7, and their relative energies are listed in Table S3.

The most stable structure in Fig. 3 (a) is denoted Complex and has a cyclic form consisting of three hydrogen bonds. The hydrogen atom (H9) of the HPHN molecule was connected to one of the oxygen atoms (O2) of CO2, denoted HB-1; another oxygen atom (O3) of CO2 is bound to one of the hydrogen atoms (H5) of H2O, denoted HB-2; another hydrogen atom (H6) of H2O is linked with the oxygen atom (O8) of HPHN, denoted HB-3.

The photophysical and photochemical processes can be dominated by the lowest-lying excited state (S1 and T1 states) based on Kasha's rule [47]. As shown in Fig. 3(b), at the S1 state, there are three competing transition processes, including radiative decay from S1 to S0, nonradiative internal conversion (IC) from S1 to S0, and the nonradiative intersystem crossing (ISC) from S1 to T1; at the T1 state, the nonradiative ISC from T1 to S0 is the only photophysical process. The formulas for the rate constants of k F, k IC, k ISC are provided in Supporting information (Computational section).

We calculated the corresponding rate constants (k F, k IC, k ISC (S1-T1) and k ISC (T1-S0)) of Complex, which are shown in Table 1 . It can be seen that at the S1 state, k IC is the largest among the three rate constants and k ISC (S1-T1) is the slowest, indicating that only a small proportion of Complex has the ability to transition to the T1 state and most of it returns to the S0 state, which is unfavorable for the chemical reactions that mainly occur at the T1 state. Then, in the photophysical process, the ISC from the S1 to T1 state is probably the rate-limiting step; at the T1 state, the only photophysical process is the ISC from T1 to S0, and its rate constant is small, which means that the molecules have a long lifetime and remain longer in the T1 state before returning to the S0 state. Then, more molecules are involved in the photochemical reactions.

The initial and key step in the process of CO2 reduction is CO2 activation, which can facilitate the subsequent reactions. To explore the behavior of CO2 activation in the photophysical process, we analyzed the relevant bond lengths, bond angle, ADCH charges and spin populations for Complex in the S0, S1 and T1 states, as shown in Table S2. Most of the parameters are unchanged and only a slightly bend is found in the structure of the CO2 moiety (the angle of O-C-O is 178°). Therefore, the activation of CO2 does not occur in the photophysical process.

At the T1 state, in addition to the nonradiative ISC from T1 to S0, Complex can also undergo photochemical reactions. We calculated three possible reaction pathways (Fig. 4 , Figs. S8 and S9) for Complex reduction of CO2. The lowest-energy reaction pathway (Path-1) in Fig. 4 was investigated. Path-1 mainly involves three steps: step 1 is involved in the generation of the intermediate COOH∙ radical, step 2 is mainly about the rupture of the C=O bond of COOH∙, and step 3 is related to the generation of the OH∙ radical.

Step 1 is the first step in reaction Path-1, which has the highest barrier of 38.3 kcal/mol. To explore the essence of the mechanism, the bond lengths, bond angle, ADCH charges and spin populations data are listed in Table 2 and Table S4. We can see that the bond length of HB-1 gradually decrease of 2.89 Å, 1.39 Å, and 1.01 Å for models 3R* (reactant), 3TS1* (transition state), and 3IM1* (intermediate), respectively, followed by the hydrogen atom (H9) of HPHN transfer to CO2 to form intermediate COOH∙; this process is also a transformation between the hydrogen bond and chemical bond. In addition, the ADCH charges of models 3R*, 3TS1*, and 3IM1* for CO2 are −0.02, −0.60, and −0.34, while for HPHN are 0.06, 0.66, and 0.41, respectively. This indicates the charge transfer from the HPHN molecule to CO2, which is accompanied by a simultaneous geometry change of CO2 from a linear configuration to a bent structure. The CO2 molecule is bent with an O-C-O angle of 128° and there is obvious elongation of the C-O bond length for models 3IM1* (1.31 Å) compared to model 3R* (1.17 Å), indicating a high degree activation of the CO2 molecule. Above all, the activation of CO2 and COOH∙ formation is attributed to the assistance of HB-1, formed between the HPHN molecule and CO2 molecule. This H-bonding CO2 adsorption mode can facilitate not only proton transfer but also electron transfer from the HPHN molecule to the CO2 molecule, which is identified as proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) induced by the hydrogen bonding in the excited state. In addition, the hydroxyl oxygen atom is the active center of this step, which is determined by hydrogen bonding. The spin density plotted in Fig. 4 also indicates that the unpaired electron is located mostly on the carbon atom in 3IM1*, meaning that C is indeed reduced in this case and COOH∙ is generated.

Step 2 is the second step in reaction Path-1 for the rupture of the C=O bond of COOH∙, which has a barrier of 36.8 kcal/mol. After the formation of radical COOH∙, a hydrogen bond is formed between the hydrogen atom (H9) of COOH∙ and oxygen atom (O8) of the HPHN in 3IM1*. The bond length of the hydrogen bond is 1.61 Å, which means that there is an interaction between COOH∙ and the HPHN, and the hydrogen bond may promote the rupture of COOH∙. Table 2 shows that the bond length of C1=O2 increase gradually of 1.31 Å, 2.45 Å and 2.94 Å for models 3IM1*, 3TS2*, and 3IM2*, respectively. Then the C1=O2 bond of the COOH∙ intermediate fractures and generates CO and OH∙ radical. It can be seen from Table S4 the spin populations in 3IM2* are almost located in the O2 of OH∙, and the spin distribution also indicates that the unpaired electron is located mostly on the oxygen atom in 3IM2*. Step 2 completes the reduction of CO2 and generates CO and OH∙.

Step 3 is the last step of reaction Path-1 and is mainly about the oxidation of H2O, which has the lowest barrier of 31.0 kcal/mol. The bond length of HB-3 in Table 2 formed by the HPHN molecule and H2O molecule, gradually decreases of 1.88 Å, 1.01 Å, and 0.98 Å, followed by the increasing of O4-H6 distance from 0.98 Å, 1.55 Å to 1.88 Å for models 3IM2*, 3TS3*, and 3P*, respectively. Then, the hydrogen atom (H6) of the H2O molecule transfers to the oxygen atom of HPHN molecule. Additionally, it is also clearly demonstrated that the catalyst HPHN can be recovered during the whole reaction pathway, which is originally composed of hydrogen H9 and ultimately replaced by hydrogen H6. The ADCH charges for HPHN are 0.51, 0.19, and 0.05 for models 3IM2*, 3TS3*, and 3P*, respectively. This indicates that the electron transfer is from the H2O molecule to the HPHN molecule, which is followed by proton transfer and is mainly through HB-3 in the excited state. Then the PCET during the oxidation of H2O were induced by HB-3 in the excited state, as in Step 1. In addition, the carbonyl oxygen atom is the active center of this step, which is also determined by the hydrogen bonding. Finally, the product 3P* consists of catalyst HPHN, CO and two OH∙ radicals, that can be further coupled to generate H2O2. Table S4 and Fig. 4 also indicate that the spin populations and the unpaired electrons in 3P* are mostly located in O2 and O4 of the two OH∙ radicals.

According to the above computed results, the formation of COOH∙ radical is identified as the rate-limiting step with the calculated barrier of 38.3 kcal/mol. The reaction rate constant (k R) of Path-1 is necessary to evaluate the photochemical process and is provided in the Supporting information (Computational section). The calculated reaction rate constant k R of Path-1 in the photochemical process is 2.05 × 10−15 s−1. Comparing the rate constants k ISC (S1-T1) (4.97 × 104 s−1) in the photophysical process, it is clear that the COOH∙ formation step of Path-1 is the ultimate rate-limiting step in the entire competition of both photophysical and photochemical processes.

The photocatalytic cycle of the hydrogen bonding induced CO2 reduction reaction is discussed in detail in our research, which is summarized in Fig. 5 , and the relevant reactions are also listed in Scheme 1 . Starting with the pristine state, catalyst HPHN undergoes a cycle of giving electron and proton to CO2 and then receiving electron and proton from H2O. We studied the photophysical and photochemical processes of the hydrogen-bonded complex, mainly related to the PCET induced by hydrogen bonding in the excited state.

Among them, steps (4), (5) and (6) are the three most important reactions, which are closely related to the hydrogen bonding in the excited state. Step (4) regards the COOH∙ radical formation, which is the hardest step in the whole process. In addition, Step (4) is mainly regards the electron transfer from the catalyst HPHN to CO2, which clarifies the importance of the electron donating ability of the catalyst HPHN. Therefore, improving the electron donating ability of the HPHN catalyst could be an effective method to improve the photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening the best metal ions using volcano plot
ce:section.ce:para: The ionization potential (IP) is an important and fundamental parameter reflecting the ability of electron donation, which is considered in our study. To screen the metal ions with the excellent performance, we chose nine divalent metal ions in the fourth cycle of the periodic table and calculated their electron-donating capabilities when interacting with the HPHN catalyst. In addition, the binding energy among the HPHN-metal, CO2 and H2O was also investigated, which indicates how strongly CO2 and H2O are bound to the catalyst. We made a volcano plot based on the consideration of those above two aspects, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The X-axis is defined as the binding energy, and an appropriate binding energy is important for the photocatalytic reactions because too large of a binding energy is unfavorable to the desorption of the intermediate and the consecutive catalytic reactions, while too small of a binding energy is unable to activate the CO2 molecule. The Y-axis is defined as the reciprocal of IP, and the larger the value of 1/IP, the stronger the ability to donate electrons. Overall, Zn2+ ions are the best candidate with the appropriate binding energy and higher electron-donating ability. Take Zn2+ ions as the example, we calculated several coordination modes of Zn2+ and HPHN, and finally obtained two different coordinate modes and listed in Table S5. The most stable structure for HPHN-Zn2+ is provided in Fig. S10(a). The computed IR spectra for HPHN-Zn2+ provided in Fig. S10(b) show the stretching modes of Zn-O bond.

The optimized hydrogen-bonded complex with the addition of Zn2+ ions is denoted Complex-Zn and its structures in the S0, S1 and T1 states is shown in Fig. S11. It is clear that the configuration of CO2 for Complex-Zn is already bent at T1 state, which means that the activation of CO2 for Complex-Zn occurs in the photophysical process. As the rate-limiting step of Complex is the COOH∙ formation in the photochemical process, we mainly calculated and investigated this key step after the addition of Zn2+ ions, as is shown in Fig. 6(b). The bond length of HB-1 between HPHN-Zn and CO2 is reduced to 1.41 Å compared to 2.89 Å for HB-1 for Complex, which is much more favorable for proton transfer from HPHN-Zn to CO2, forming the COOH∙ intermediate. At this COOH∙ formation step, Zn atom is the active center and the barrier of Complex-Zn is 0.9 kcal/mol, which is noticeably decreased compared with that of Complex (38.3 kcal/mol). This indicates that the participation of Zn2+ ions is efficacious in the COOH∙ formation step. The calculated results show the excellent electron-donating ability of Zn2+ ions and confirmed that our prediction is reasonable. The reaction pathways of the COOH∙ formation step for the other eight Complex-metals were also calculated and shown in Fig. S12 and the corresponding structures of 3R*, 3TS1* and 3IM1* of them are listed in Fig. S13.

As discussed before, the calculations provide evidence that the rate-limiting step of Complex is significantly facilitated by the addition of Zn2+ ions; therefore, the addition of Zn2+ ions could be an effective way to improve the activity of CO2 reduction. To verify the calculated results, we performed experimental verification, the results are shown in Fig. 2(f). The activity of HPHN-CQDs-Zn2+ is 166.6 μmol/gcat., which is 2.9 times the activity of HPHN-CQDs (58.1 μmol/gcat.). It is clear that the addition of Zn2+ ions can greatly improve the photocatalytic activity of CO2 reduction, which proves that our calculation is reasonable. In addition, we also performed the experiment on the photocatalytic activity of Ni2+ ions in the volcano plot for CO2 reduction. Fig. 2(f) shows that the photocatalytic activity of HPHN-CQDs-Ni2+ is less than that of HPHN-CQDs-Zn2+, which is consistent with results of the electron-donating ability in the volcano plot. Therefore, we can conclude that, according to our calculations, the prediction of the volcano plot is reliable and has been verified by the experiment.

In summary, we presented a complete picture of the CO2 reduction mechanism for the model catalyst HPHN-CQDs. Our focus is mainly on the mechanism of the proton and electron transfer induced by the hydrogen bonding in the excited state. The calculated results of the photophysical and photochemical processes show that the formation of COOH∙ is the rate-limiting step, which is closely related to the PCET induced by the hydrogen bonding in the excited state. And the hydroxyl oxygen atom is the active center of COOH∙ formation step. The prediction we provided is addition of Zn2+ ions according to the volcano plot. After the addition of Zn2+ ions, the active center changed from the hydroxyl oxygen atom to the Zn atom and the barrier of the rate-limiting step was decreased. Furthermore, the experimental results show that the activity of CO2 reduction is greatly improved when Zn2+ ions are added. The fundamental study in our work used a combination of experimental and theoretical methods, which together provide thorough comprehension of the reaction mechanism. Understanding and characterizing the essence and mechanism of the reaction will open new vistas and lay a strong foundation for our knowledge of photocatalytic CO2 reduction, which could also be helpful for designing and synthesizing new and better photocatalytic systems with higher efficiency.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21677029, 21606040) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (DUT18LK26).
